content.body:
Annex K update:
- it applies to protocols, not just algorithms
- there is a new “iii” case allowing manufacturers to do some kind of risk assessment on algorithms/protocols not listed in the vertical or ACM
- the adaptation of Annex K to the verticals will be non-trivial, requiring significant work on our part (not just listing the algorithms, though we definitely need to do that)
- there is another meeting of the task force tomorrow if anyone wants to attend
content.formatted_body:
Annex K update:

  • it applies to protocols, not just algorithms
  • there is a new “iii” case allowing manufacturers to do some kind of risk assessment on algorithms/protocols not listed in the vertical or ACM
  • the adaptation of Annex K to the verticals will be non-trivial, requiring significant work on our part (not just listing the algorithms, though we definitely need to do that)
  • there is another meeting of the task force tomorrow if anyone wants to attend

content.body: Overall we need to do a bunch of editorial work to land all the patches, bring them to a consistent style and write the assessments. This should be somehow “rote” and “easy”… hopefully possible to do during this week

Let’s really sprint to get a complete draft by May 31st. Releasing a complete draft will enable us to distribute it to all the browsers for feedback, since it’s harder to give feedback on individual patches.
Sounds like the TLS requirements should cite ETSI EN 319 411-1 which, it is claimed, has alignment with browsers and CA/Browser forum
I don't think EN 319 411-1 covers TLS, only how to deploy/run a PKI system.
Well, we could mention it when discussing the root cert list

See you all in a couple hours to discuss the remaining gaps and any further work to integrate patches. The URL of the meeting might be sent out at the last minute, and i will share it here when available

I asked Camille about the options between normatively referencing unversioned documents vs making documentation-driven requirements, and she said she'd prefer the normative references. So maybe we should be referencing HTML! I'm not sure. She invited us to have a follow-on conversation.
she's also talked about how it would be preferable to refer to specific clauses of documents, where possible.
* she's also talked about how it would be preferable to refer to specific clauses of documents, where possible. This might also be difficult.

m.new_content.body: she's also talked about how it would be preferable to refer to specific clauses of documents, where possible. This might also be difficult.

content.body: About the question of whether distribution of security functions should include things like per-site data clearing, Camille said it did seem to be in scope, and suggested looking at Network Interfaces for a good example of the distribution of security functions section.

* About the question of whether distribution of security functions should include things like per-site data clearing (which could help websites meet CRA requirements), Camille said it did seem to be in scope, and suggested looking at Network Interfaces for a good example of the distribution of security functions section.

m.new_content.body: About the question of whether distribution of security functions should include things like per-site data clearing (which could help websites meet CRA requirements), Camille said it did seem to be in scope, and suggested looking at Network Interfaces for a good example of the distribution of security functions section.

content.body: Neither were critical, but seemed like things that 1) require broader discussion because 2) the decision would have document-wide impact.
Eg: are definitions high level and simple? or exhaustive and complex?
